coredata.dc:title: Luminescence color regulation of carbon quantum dots by surface modification
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jing
Wang, Wei
An, Bao-Li
Jia, Xin
Zhang, Ying-Hao
Li, Juan-Rong
Bai, Yue-Ling
Xu, Jia-Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Labelling various biological molecules with multi-color carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in the same scene has great potential application for investigating the relationship between those molecules. It is still a great challenge to regulate the color of CQDs. Ample –NH2 groups on the surface of green carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) were characterized and confirmed by 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) data. As–NH2 groups had high organic reactivity, the surface of GCQDs was designed and modified with several organic functional groups through a simple surface modification strategy of organic reactions. The surface band gap states of GCQDs were tuned gradually by the corresponding modified functional groups, and the emission colors of GCQDs were tuned from green to yellow-green, then to orange, and finally to red. All the emission spectra for these multicolor carbon quantum dots (M-CQDs) were excitation independent, and the lifetimes for the M-CQDs were mainly determined by the second electron decay process from the surface defect band states. The red-shift colors for the M-CQDs were ascribed to the emissions from their surface defect band states, and the luminescence mechanism had been investigated. This work opens up a novel synthesis strategy to regulate the emission colors of carbon quantum dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Emission color regulation
Surface modification
Luminescence
Synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the surface groups of the M-CQDs
The quantum yields and the lifetimes
The regulation mechanism of the emission color

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis process for the M-CQDs.
The TEM and HRTEM (inset) images and particle size distribution diagrams of GCQD-NBS (a, e), GCQD-PCS (b, f), GCQD-NBC (c, g) and GCQD-DAB (d, h).
1H NMR spectrum for GCQDs (400 MHz, CDCl3).
FT-IR spectra of GCQDs, GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB in the range of 4000 cm−1–400 cm−1.
UV–vis absorption spectra of dilute isopropanol solutions of the M-CQDs and GCQDs.
The determination of the band gap energies for (a) GCQD-NBS, (b) GCQD-PCS, (c) GCQD-NBC and (d) GCQD-DAB from their UV–vis spectra.

(a) The normalized emission spectra of GCQDs, GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB. (b) The CIE color coordinates for the M-CQDs and (c) the diagram of the relation between the color coordinates and the lowest excited surface defect band states. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) The emission spectrum of GCQD-PCS (λex = 365 nm). (b) The curves for the lifetime values of τ 1 and τ 2 of GCQD-PCS solution versus the detection wavelengths using a 375 nm laser. (c) The curves for the lifetime values of τ 1 and τ 2 of GCQD-NBS versus the temperatures using a 450 nm laser.
The regulation mechanism of the emission color for the M-CQDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The quantum yield data for GCQDs and the M-CQD.
The lifetime data for GCQDs and the M-CQDs using a 450 nm laser.

head.ce:title: Luminescence color regulation of carbon quantum dots by surface modification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, Shanghai University, Shanghai, 200444, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Labelling various biological molecules with multi-color carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in the same scene has great potential application for investigating the relationship between those molecules. It is still a great challenge to regulate the color of CQDs. Ample –NH2 groups on the surface of green carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) were characterized and confirmed by 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) data. As–NH2 groups had high organic reactivity, the surface of GCQDs was designed and modified with several organic functional groups through a simple surface modification strategy of organic reactions. The surface band gap states of GCQDs were tuned gradually by the corresponding modified functional groups, and the emission colors of GCQDs were tuned from green to yellow-green, then to orange, and finally to red. All the emission spectra for these multicolor carbon quantum dots (M-CQDs) were excitation independent, and the lifetimes for the M-CQDs were mainly determined by the second electron decay process from the surface defect band states. The red-shift colors for the M-CQDs were ascribed to the emissions from their surface defect band states, and the luminescence mechanism had been investigated. This work opens up a novel synthesis strategy to regulate the emission colors of carbon quantum dots.

The emission colors of the green carbon quantum dots were tuned from green to red by a surface modification strategy of organic reactions, and the maximum emission wavelengths were tuned from 507 nm to 525 nm, 545 nm, 608 nm, 629 nm step by step.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots have been intensively investigated owing to their outstanding properties including highly tunable photoluminescence (PL) property, high thermal stability, photostability, good biocompatibility and surface modification flexibility [1–3]. CQDs are quasi-spherical fluorescent nanoparticles with particle size of less than 10 nm, which are composed of carbon nuclei with various functional groups on the surface [4]. CQDs can be synthesized by diversity of synthetic methods and processes [5]. Pyrolysis, carbonization and chemical fusion have been extensively applied to synthesize CQDs using organic small molecules or oligomers as raw materials [6]. At present, hydrothermal and solvothermal methods are the most widely used to synthesize CQDs together with more uniform particle size and distribution than those prepared by microwave and micro-plasma synthesis methods [7]. Excitingly, researchers have synthesized blue, green, yellow, and red CQDs through hydrothermal and solvothermal methods, and the quantum yields (QY) for green and red carbon quantum dots have increased to 90% and 80% respectively [8,9].

Recently, CQDs have been widely used in sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery, photo catalysis and light-emitting diodes [10–14]. It is presently necessary to synthesize CQDs with multiple colors for application in multi-color bio-image and multi-color display. Researchers hope to label different biological organs with various colors of luminescent nanoparticles in order to distinguish different organs in the same scene, and this technology can be also carried out to study medical treatments [15]. White light emitting diodes (WLEDs) are proposed to be fabricated by combining red, green and blue carbon quantum dots as candidate luminescent materials. The most commonly used materials for fabricating WLEDs are rare earth luminescent materials which are usually expensive [16]. Thus, CQDs are considered to be more environmentally materials for WLEDs together with low cost.

Two luminescence mechanisms for CQDs are extensively accepted. One mechanism is that the luminescence comes from the band gap states based on the conjugated π-domains in the carbon nucleus of the CQDs, and the other is that the luminescence comes from the surface-related defect band states of the CQDs [17], which can cause surface energy traps [18]. Generally, the color regulation strategies for CQDs mainly include selecting carbon source of chemicals with different degrees of conjugation, changing the polarity of the solvent [19], adjusting the molar ratio of raw chemicals, and controlling the reaction temperature and time [20]. Surface groups strongly affect the properties of CQDs, and they can be introduced via covalent and noncovalent modification. Amide coupling reactions (CQDs–CONH–R, or CQDs–NHCO–R) are the mostly reported covalent modification [21]. The amino groups on the surfaces of CQDs were dehydrated with the carboxy groups from decanoic acid and perfluorodecanoic acid respectively, and the red color of the CQDs in methanol was tuned from red to green and violet respectively. The blue-shift emission was ascribed to the strong electron-withdrawing group surface modification of the CQDs [22]. The color was modulated from blue to red by introducing hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium cations on the surfaces of the CQDs through the electrostatic interactions with -COO- anions [23]. Ren's team firstly characterized the groups on the surface of CQDs using 1H NMR [24]. Presently, it is still a challenge to tune the emission color of CQDs with both long emission wavelength and high QY. However, in our knowledge surface modification strategy has not been reported to tune the emission color of carbon quantum dots. In our previous work, SnO2 nano particles have been confirmed having great catalysis for the pyrolysis of two chemicals as carbon and nitrogen atom sources, and bright yellow carbon quantum dots with absolute quantum yield of 38% were synthesized using solvothermal method in 2 h [25]. Green carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) with ample –NH2 groups on the surfaces were synthesized in 4 h using SnO2 nano particles as a catalyst. Moreover, GCQDs could be uniformly dispersed in water solution or some organic solvents such as ethanol, isopropyl alcohol, tetrahydrofuran and methylbenzene. Moreover, GCQDs could be used to detect NO2 − in water or food with high sensitivity [20].

In this work, taking advantage of the high reactivity of –NH2 groups, several functional groups are designed to modify the surfaces of GCQDs by covalent bonding in order to tune the emission color of the carbon quantum dots. The surfaces of GCQDs have been successfully modified using N-bromosuccinimide (NBS), p-chloromethylstyrene (PCS), 4-nitrobenzyl chloride (NBC) and 4-dimethylaminobenzaldehyde (DAB) as reactants, and the maximum emission wavelengths for GCQDs were tuned from 507 nm to 525 nm, 545 nm, 608 nm, 629 nm gradually. For simplicity, the surface-modified GCQDs were named as GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC, GCQD-DAB correspondingly, and they emitted bright green, yellow-green, orange, and red luminescence respectively. The surface organic groups for the GCQDs and the M-CQDs were characterized using 1H NMR spectra. The regulation mechanism of the emission color was investigated through the surface band gap energies of the M-CQDs which were tuned by the bonding surface groups.

Ethanol (99.7%), 4-dimethylaminobenzaldehyde (99.0%, DAB), isopropyl alcohol (HPLC grade), tin (IV) chloride pentahydrate (99.0%) and m-phenylenediamine (99.5%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. of China. Potassium carbonate and potassium bromide which were analytical grade were purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Analytical grade 4-nitrobenzyl chloride (99.0%, NBC) was purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. N-bromosuccinimide (99.0%, NBS) was purchased from Jiangsu Qiangsheng Functional Chemical Co., Ltd. p-chloromethylstyrene (90.0%, PCS) was purchased from Shanghai Xianding Biological Technology Co., Ltd. All the chemicals were used directly without further purification.

GCQDs were synthesized and purified by our reported solvothermal method using simultaneously synthesized tin dioxide nanoparticles as a catalyst [20]. In our previous work, the Sn element was not found in GCQDs according to the XPS spectrum, so SnO2 nano particles only acted as a catalyst in the pyrolysis reaction. And the final GCQDs solution in isopropanol (25 ml) was stored in refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

5.0 ml GCQDs solution was dispersed in a mixture of 30.0 ml isopropyl alcohol and 10.0 ml deionized water. Then, 3.0 mmol K2CO3 was added to GCQDs solution, and the solution was stirred in N2 atmosphere for 30 min. 2.3 mmol NBS in isopropyl alcohol solution was injected into the mixed solution, and it was continuously stirred at 80 °C. The reaction product was monitored by silicon dioxide thin layer chromatography (TLC) until the –NH2 groups on the surface of GCQDs were completely reacted (about 2 days). The polarity of GCQDs was reduced after being modified by NBS. The eluting agent was a mixed solution of petroleum ether and ethyl acetate (V/V = 7:3), and the retention factor (R f) values for GCQDs and GCQD-NBS were 0.32 and 0.89 respectively. After evaporating the solvent by a rotary evaporator, the syrup-like product was dispersed in 25 ml isopropanol. The mixed solution was filtrated to remove the undissolved potassium carbonate and other larger solid particles. Bright green carbon quantum dots solution was obtained, which was named as GCQD-NBS.

GCQD-PCS was synthesized using a similar method as that for GCQD-NBS just replacing NBS with PCS. With the same eluting agent as that for GCQD-NBS, the R f value for GCQD-PCS was 0.88. The –NH2 groups on the surface of GCQDs were completely reacted in about 24 h. Bright yellow-green carbon quantum dots solution was obtained, which was named as GCQD-PCS.

GCQD-NBC was synthesized using a similar method as that for GCQD-NBS, just replacing NBS with NBC. With the same eluting agent as that for GCQD-NBS, the R f value for GCQD-NBC was 0.66. The –NH2 groups on the surface of GCQDs were completely reacted in about 26 h. Bright orange carbon quantum dots solution was obtained, which was named as GCQD-NBC.

The solvent of 5.0 ml GCQDs solution was evaporated by a rotary evaporator, and the resulting product was dispersed into 5.0 ml ethanol (solution A). 1.0 mmol DAB was dissolved into 5 ml ethanol solution (solution B). Then, solution B was added slowly into solution An under magnetic stirring, and the mixed solution was continuously stirred at room temperature. The silicon dioxide thin layer chromatography was used to monitor the products until the –NH2 groups on the surface of GCQDs were completely reacted in about 24 h. With the same eluting agent as that for GCQD-NBS, the R f value for GCQD-DAB was 0.59. Bright red carbon quantum dots solution was obtained, which was named as GCQD-DAB.
The synthesis scheme for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

1H NMR spectra were recorded on JEOL-400MHz. 1H NMR chemical shifts were reported in δ units, parts per million (ppm) relative to the chemical shift of residual solvent. Reference peak for chloroform in 1H NMR spectra was set at 7.26 ppm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) micrographs were performed using transmission electron microscope (TEM-2100F). The test was carried out at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in the range of 4000 cm−1–400 cm−1 were obtained on a Thermo Nicolet AVATRA 370 infrared spectrophotometer by conventional KBr method. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were recorded on an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Pulsing TU-1950) at room temperature. The excitation and emission spectra were measured by a transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, FLS 1000). The time-resolved fluorescence decay curves for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS and GCQD-NBC were recorded from Edinburgh Instrument of FLS 1000 with EPL-Visible 450 nm laser as the excitation light, and the time-resolved decay curve for GCQD-DAB was recorded from the same fluorescence spectrometer with EPL-Visible 480 nm laser as the excitation light. All the lifetime values were obtained by auto calculation of the same fluorescence spectrometer from the corresponding time-resolved decay curves. The absolute quantum yields of M-CQDs were determined on Edinburgh Instrument of FLS 1000 with a Red PMT sphere, and all the quantum yield values were obtained by auto calculation by this instrument.

The morphologies and size distributions for GCQDs were reported in our presently published work. The XRD pattern of GCQDs showed a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 25.9°, which is attributed to the (002) diffraction patterns of the graphitic carbon [20]. The morphologies and size distributions for the M-CQDs are given in Fig. 2 . All the M-CQDs showed good dispersibility without obvious aggregation, and the particles were close to quasi-spherical. The insets inserted in Fig. 2a-d showed the HRTEM images of the M-CQDs. The in-plane lattice spaces for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB were all 0.21 nm, which was ascribed to the (100) planes of graphitic carbon [26]. The M-CQDs had the same lattice spaces of GCQDs, indicating that they all had similar carbon core structures as that of GCQDs. Compared with that for GCQDs, the average size for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB was increased to 3.4 nm, 3.5 nm, 3.9 nm and 4.6 nm respectively, and the size distributions for the M-CQDs were improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the surface groups of the M-CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: Ample –NH2 and –CH3 groups on the surfaces of GCQDs have been confirmed by FT-IR and XPS spectra analysis [20]. Taking account of the complexity of the surface groups of GCQDs, it is a challenge to characterize the surface groups of GCQDs completely and accurately. 1H NMR spectrum for GCQDs was recorded in CDCl3 solution in order to further characterize the surface groups of GCQDs (Fig. 3 a). The 1H NMR full survey spectrum of GCQDs was divided into three regions which were named as region A, B and C respectively. Region A was ascribed to a similar structure of m-phenylenediamine bonding on the surfaces of GCQDs (Fig. 3b). The NMR peaks of H1 and H2 were split into triplet and doublet respectively by the spin coupling of their ortho-protons. Each peak for the doublet of H2 was further split into doublet by the spin coupling of meta-protons of H3 (4 J = 2.4 Hz). And the NMR peak for H3 was split into triplet by the spin coupling of the meta-protons of H2 (4 J = 2.2 Hz). Moreover, the integral area ratio of H1, H2 and H3 was 1:2:1.

Region B was attributed to a similar structure of –CHCH–CH3 bonding on the surfaces of GCQDs (Fig. 3b). The NMR peaks for the olefin hydrogen H1 and H2 were split into quintuplet and triplet respectively by the spin coupling of their corresponding ortho-protons. Moreover, the integral area ratio of the olefin hydrogen H1 and H2 was 1:1, which confirmed the presence of group of –CHCH–CH3 on the surfaces of GCQDs. The number of –CHCH–CH3 group was deduced to be 6 times of that of the –NH2 group.

Region C was attributed to isopropyl (-CH(CH3)2) and ethyl (-CH2CH3) groups bonding on the surfaces of GCQDs (Fig. 3b). In Fig. 3b, triplet 1 and doublet peak 2 were ascribed to the methyl NMR peaks from the ethyl and the isopropyl groups respectively. The multiplet 3 and 4 were attributed to the NMR peaks from the methylene of the ethyl and the methyne of the isopropyl groups respectively. Moreover, based on the integral area data, the molar ratio of –NH2, –CHCH- and –CH3 groups was deduced to be around 1: 6: 16 on the surfaces of GCQDs. The total 1H NMR data (400 MHz, CDCl3) for GCQDs are shown as following: δ 6.94 (t, 3 J = 7.9 Hz, 1H), 6.12 (d, d, 3 J = 8.0, 4 J = 2.4 Hz, 2H), 6.05 (t, 4 J = 2.2 Hz, 1H), 4.03 (m, 3 J = 6.0 Hz, 6H), 3.00 (t, 3 J = 8.0 Hz, 6H), 1.76 (m, 3 J = 7.0, 10H), 1.44 (m, 3 J = 7.2 Hz, 10H), 1.21 (d, 3 J = 4.0 Hz, 37H), 0.95 (t, 3 J = 8.0 Hz, 11H).

The 1H NMR spectra for the M-CQDs are shown in the supporting information (Fig. S1). As shown in Fig. S1a, compared with the characteristic 1H NMR at δ 2.97 (s, 4H) for pure NBS [27], the 1H NMR singlet value for GCQD-NBS was reduced to δ 2.74 (s, 4H). The NMR data for GCQD-NBS confirmed successful surface modification of GCQDs by NBS. GCQD-NBS: 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ 2.74 (s, 1H). Compared with the 1H NMR δ values for pure PCS [28], all the δ values for GCQD-PCS decreased variety of 0.06–1.09 ppm, which definitely confirmed the successful surface modification of GCQDs by PCS (Fig. S1b). GCQD-PCS: 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ 7.32 (m, 4H), 6.69 (d, d, 3 J = 20.0, 3 J = 12.0 Hz, 1H), 5.71 (d, d, 3 J = 20.0, 4 J = 4.0 Hz, 1H), 5.19 (d, d, 3 J = 12.0, 4 J = 0.8 Hz, 1H), 3.51 (s, 2H). Compared with the 1H NMR δ values for pure NBC [29], all the δ values for GCQD-NBC decreased variety of 0.03–0.89 ppm, which definitely confirmed the successful surface modification of GCQDs by NBC (Fig. S1c). GCQD-NBC: 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ 8.18 (d, d, 3 J = 8.0, 4 J = 4.0 Hz, 2H), 7.53 (m, 2H), 3.89 (s, 1H), 3.65 (s, 1H). The 1H chemical shift values for GCQD-DAB were similar with the corresponding values for pure DAB [30]. However, the split chemical shift of 3 J (8.0 Hz) for GCQD-DAB at δ 7.74 was lower than the 3 J (9.0 Hz) for pure DAB, and the split chemical shift of 3 J (12.0 Hz) for GCQD-DAB at δ 6.71 was larger than the 3 J (9.0 Hz) for pure DAB (Fig. S1d). GCQD-DAB: 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ 9.74 (s, 1H), 7.74 (m, 2H), 6.71 (m, 2H), 3.09 (s, 6H).

The FT-IR spectra for GCQDs, GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB are given in Fig. 4 in order to further characterize the groups on the surfaces of the M-CQDs. The FT-IR spectra for all the M-CQDs and GCQDs showed strong characteristic absorption peaks of methyl groups at 2974 cm−1 (νas, C–H, –CH3), 2888 cm−1 (νs, C–H, –CH3) and 1383 cm−1 (δ s, C–H, –CH3) together with medium absorption peaks at 1464 cm−1 (δ as, C–H, –CH3). Since C–N and C–C are polar and non-polar bonds respectively, the absorption peaks of C–N bonds are generally stronger than that of C–C bonds and the wavenumbers of the C–N bond are generally lesser than that of the C–C bonds [20]. The FT-IR spectra for all the M-CQDs and GCQDs showed strong vibration absorptions of the C–N bonds at 1151 cm−1 and 954 cm−1, together with vibration absorptions of the C–C bonds at 1248 cm−1. Compared with the weak narrow peak at 3655 cm−1 (ν , N–H) from the –NH2 groups of GCQDs, a new weak narrow peak at 3752 cm−1 (ν , N–H) was observed for all the M-CQDs. It was deduced that there were two kinds of –NH– groups on the surfaces of the M-CQDs, and the structure changes of the –NH– groups were engendered by the substitution reaction of –NH2 group with NBS, PCS, NBC and DAB respectively.

Compared with the original FT-IR spectrum for GCQDs, there was a new strong peak at 1715 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum for GCQD-NBS, which was ascribed to the stretching vibration absorption from the CO of NBS. Besides, the new weak peak at 1556 cm−1 for GCQD-NBS was ascribed to the skeleton vibration of CC indicating that there might be partial enol form of NBS. The FT-IR data for GCQD-NBS confirmed successful surface modification of GCQDs by NBS, which was in accordance with the result from the NMR data for GCQD-NBS.

However, the spectrum for GCQD-PCS was nearly identical to that for GCQDs except the new narrow peak at 3752 cm−1 as vibrational absorption peak of –NH– groups for GCQD-PCS. The reason was attributed to that the absorptions of the stretch vibration of fewer –CC–H on the benzene ring and the vinyl groups were much weaker than that for the ample methyl groups.

The FT-IR spectrum for GCQD-NBC showed two medium absorption peaks at 1345 cm−1 and 1522 cm−1, which were attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration absorptions of –NO2 respectively [31]. Moreover, the vibration absorption at 1611 cm−1 from the aromatic ring structure was observed. Two new weak absorption peaks at 690 cm−1 and 737 cm−1 were ascribed to the out-of-plane bending vibration absorption of the C–H on the benzene ring, which further confirmed the successful modification of GCQD by NBC [32].

Four characteristic absorption peaks at 1659 cm−1, 1596 cm−1, 1542 cm−1 and 1464 cm−1 from the aromatic hydrocarbons were observed in the FT-IR spectrum for GCQD-DAB. In addition, the weak peak at 727 cm−1 was ascribed to the out-of-plane bending vibration absorption of the C–H on benzene ring, which further confirmed the successful modification of GCQDs by DAB.

The UV–vis absorption spectra for GCQDs and all the M-CQDs are shown in Fig. 5 . Three prominent peaks centered at 257 nm, 294 nm and 460 nm were observed on the spectrum for GCQDs. The peaks at 257 nm and 294 nm were ascribed to the π–π* transitions while the peak at 460 nm was assigned to the electron transitions of the surface defect band states [20]. Compared with that for GCQDs, the π–π* absorption peaks for the M-CQDs shifted from 257 nm to 294 nm–272 nm and 356 nm respectively, which was attributed to that larger conjugated structures of the surface groups for the M-CQDs had been formed [33]. In addition, GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB had sufficient absorptions at 384 nm, 405 nm, 460 nm and 485 nm respectively. The red shift of the absorption peaks confirmed that the surface defect band states for the M-CQDs had been regulated gradually through the surface modification of GCQDs with NBS, PCS, NBC and DAB as functional groups respectively, and the emission properties of the M-CQDs were further regulated.

The direct band gap energies for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB were determined by the following equation [34].
(3-1)
(
ε h
ν
)
2
= B
(
h v −
E g
)
where ε is molar absorption coefficient which is equal to A/bc, A is the absorbance, b is the width of the cuvette (cm) and c is the molar concentration. The h is the Planck constant, v is the photon's frequency, E g is the band gap energy, and B is a constant. The UV absorption spectra for the M-CQDs were converted in Fig. 6 based on equations (3)–(1). The band gap energies for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB were determined to be 2.58 eV, 2.44 eV, 2.23 eV and 2.21 eV respectively. In short, the band gap energies for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB were reduced gradually by the strategy of surface modification of organic groups on the surfaces of GCQDs.

The excitation and emission spectra for the GCQDs and the M-CQDs are given in the supporting information of Fig. S2. As shown in Fig. S2a ∼ S2e, the maximum excitation wavelengths for GCQDs, GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB were 460 nm, 485 nm, 460 nm, 528 nm and 563 nm respectively. The maximum emission wavelengths for GCQDs, GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB were 507 nm, 525 nm, 545 nm, 608 nm, and 629 nm respectively. And as the synthesis time was increased from 2 h to 8 h, the maximum emission wavelengths for GCQDs remained the same, which indicating that GCQDs were not semiconductors and the luminescence color mainly depended on the surface band gap states [20]. The emission color for graphene quantum dots depends on the average size based on the calculation results using density-functional theory method [35]. Since the average size of carbon dots generally increased with the increasing synthesis time at higher temperature. The GCQD-NBS was surface modifid at 80 °C, the synthesized GCQD-NBS solutions were further reacted in an autoclave at 80 °C for 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h and 10 h respectively, and the results are given in the supporting information Fig. S3. The maximum emission wavelengths for GCQD-NBS were indepndent on the reaction time as same as the luminescence properties for GCQDs. The results indicated that the emission color mainly related to their surface defect band states. Meanwhile, the emission intensity of GCQD-NBS reached to the maximum value as the reaction time was 6 h, then it declined as the reaction time over 6 h (Fig. S3b). The emission intensity for GCQD-NBS related to the average size. Similar to that for GCQD-NBS, the emission colors for the M-CQDs were mainly dependent on their surface defect band states.

Moreover, the maximum emission wavelengths for GCQDs, GCQD-NBS and GCQD-PCS remained nearly invariable as the excitation wavelength was increased from 360 nm to 470 nm gradually (Fig. S4a ∼ S4c). Meanwhile, the maximum emission wavelength for GCQD-NBC also remained invariable with the increasing excitation wavelength from 460 nm to 550 nm (Fig. S4d). The emisson peak for GCQD-DAB showed the same excitatation-independent property as the excitation wavelength was increased from 530 nm to 580 nm (Fig. S4e). The excitation-independent photoluminescence properties for GCQDs and the M-CQDs were attributed to the emission from the relatively uniform surface defect states [36,37].

As shown in Fig. S5a, as the temperature was increased from 283 K to 353 K, the maximum intensities for the GCQD-NBS declined slightly. Moreover, the maximum emission wavelengths were red shifted from 525 nm to 530 nm, it was ascribed to the vibration broadening of the surface excited band states of GCQD-NBS at higher temperature. Interestingly, the maximum emission intensities for the GCQD-NBS decayed linearly versus the temperature in the range from 303 K to 353 K (R 2 = 0.9975, Fig. S5b). The maximum emission intensity for the GCQD-NBS at 353 K remained 71% compared with that at 298 K, showing its good thermal stability.The normalized emission spectra for GCQDs and the M-CQDs are given in Fig. 7 a. The CIE coordinates for GCQDs and the M-CQDs were calculated from their corresponding emission spectra, and the results were shown in Fig. 7b. It was seen that the CIE coordinates for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB were tuned gradually from green (GCQDs) to pure green, to yellow-green, then to orange, and finally to red region. The diagram of the relation between the color coordinates and the lowest excited surface defect band states for the M-CQDs is given in Fig. 7c. Surprisingly, the CIE-y color coordinate values for the GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB decreased nearly linearly versus the CIE-x color coordinate values as their emission colors were tuned from green to red. Consistent with their red-shift emission wavelengths, the color coordinates for the M-CQDs moved gradually from (0.3432, 0.6194) to (0.6632, 0.3366), and the corresponding the lowest excited surface defect band states decreased gradually from 2.36 ev to 1.97 ev (Fig. 7c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quantum yields and the lifetimes
ce:section.ce:para: The absolute quantum yield values for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB were determined to be 21.53%, 21.23%, 54.98% and 26.19% respectively, and the results were given in Table 1 .

Generally, as the non-radiative rate is smaller, the lifetime is longer together with the higher quantum yield. The luminescence lifetimes were determined by recording the lifetime decay curves at the maximum wavelengths at 525 nm, 545 nm, 608 nm and 629 nm for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB respectively, and their luminescence decay curves were given in the supporting information (Fig. S6). All the lifetime values were obtained by auto-calculation with the fluorescence instrument of FSL 1000, the lifetime data were given in Table 2 . All the decay curves were fitted perfectly in second exponential decay equation, and the equation was given in equations (3-2).
(3-2)
I =
I 0
+
A 1
e
(
− t /
τ 1
)
+
A 2
e
(
− t /
τ 2
)
where, I is luminescent intensity, τ 1 and τ 2 are the first lifetime and the second lifetime respectively. A 1 and A
2 are the percentages for the first decay lifetime and the second lifetime respectively. Each of the emission decay curves was composed with a short-lived component τ 1 and a long-lived component τ 2.

In order to investigate the photophysics, the lifetime decay curves for GCQD-PCS were recorded at 446 nm, 504 nm and 545 nm respectively using a 375 nm laser (Supporting information, Fig. S7). All the three curves were fitted perfectly in second exponential decay equation as given in equations (3)–(2), and the lifetime data were given in the supporting information of Table S1. The τ 2 value increased 1.42 times as the detecting wavelength from 446 nm to 545 nm, however, the τ 1 value decreased 44.2% as the detecting wavelength from 446 nm to 545 nm (Fig. 8 b). In turn, the percentage A 1 for τ 1 decreased from 20.46% to 1.23% as the detecting wavelengths from 446 nm to 545 nm, and the percentage A 2 for τ 2 increased from 79.54% to 98.71% as the detecting wavelength from 446 nm to 545 nm (Supporting information, Table S1).

The lifetime decay curves for GCQD-NBS at different temperature were recorded at 525 nm respectively using a 450 nm laser. All the decay curves were fitted perfectly in second exponential decay equation as given in equations (3–2), and the lifetime data were given in the supporting information of Table S2. Generally, since the non-radiative decay ratio increased with increased temperature, the lifetime values declined with increasing temperature. The τ 2 value decreased rapidly as the temperature increased from 298 K to 308 K, moreover, the τ 2 value remained stable in the range of 308 K–338 K. However, the τ 1 value decreased rapidly as the temperature increased from 318 K to 328 K, and the τ 1 value decreased slightly from 328 K–338 K (Fig. 8c). Compared with the temperature-dependent property of τ 1, the τ 2 value was more sensitive as the temperature was below 308 K. Since the surface groups were collided by the solvent molecules with facility as the temperature increased, the surface excited band states were sensitive to the temperature.

Based on the results from Fig. 8b and c, it was inferred that the τ 2 value mainly related to the excited surface defect band states, and the τ 1 value mainly related to the excited band states of the carbon cores. The luminescence lifetimes for the M-CQDs were mainly determined by the τ 2 values (Table 2), and the luminescence centers for the M-CQDs were infered to be surface defect band states instead of carbon cores [38]. As shown in Tables 1 and 2, the average lifetime and the quantum yield for GCQD-NBC reached the maximum among the data for the M-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The regulation mechanism of the emission color
ce:section.ce:para: The lifetime data confirmed that the luminescence for the M-CQDs mainly came from their excited surface defect band states (also named as surface gap energy states) [39], which were gradually regulated by the surface modification reactions of GCQDs. Since the excited surface defect band states for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB were tuned to decrease gradually through the surface modification of GCQDs with increasing conjugation structure of the corresponding organic groups (Fig. 6). The emisson colors were successfully tuned from green to red. Since the red-shift emission phenomenon is consistent with the red-shift excitonic absorption bands, the emissions for the M-CQDs were ascribed to the optical electron transitions from their corresponding lowest excited surface defect band states (S 1). In order to understand concisely the principle for the red shift emission of the M-CQDs, the regulation mechanism is shown in Fig. 9 .

S 0 is the ground state, S n is the excited surface defect band states, and S 1 is the lowest excited surface defect band state. A typical luminescence mechanism was illustrated with GCQD-NBS as an example. As GCQD-NBS firstly absorbed 2.58 eV excitation light, the electron transferred from the ground state (S 0) to the excited surface defect band states (S n). Then, the electron decayed non-radiatively to the lowest excited surface defect band state (S 1). Finally, as the electron transferred to the ground state, green luminescence (525 nm) emission was observed. The luminescence mechanism for GCQD-NBS is shown in the follows:
G C Q D - N B S
→
a b s o r b
2.58
e V
S n
→
N o n - r a d i a t i v e
d e c a y
S 1
→
525
n m
R a d i a t i v e
d e c a y
S 0

The energy difference between the excited surface defect band states and the lowest excited surface defect band state is the Stroke’ shift. As the lowest excited surface defect band states were decreased gradually, the emission colors for the M-CQDs were tuned gradually from green to red.

The emission color of GCQDs was tuned gradally to be pure green, green-yellow, orange and red by a one-step surface modification organic reaction, and the maximum emission wavelengths for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB were 525 nm, 545 nm, 608 nm and 629 nm respectively. The emission spectra for both GCQDs and the M-CQDs were excitation independent. The lifetimes for the M-CQDs were mainly determined by the second electron decay process from the excited surface defect band states which were tuned gradally by a simple surface modification of GCQDs. The red-shift colors for all the M-CQDs came from their excited surface defect band states. The surface band gap energies for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB were determined to be 2.58 eV, 2.44 eV, 2.23 eV and 2.21 eV respectively. The absolute quantum yields for GCQD-NBS, GCQD-PCS, GCQD-NBC and GCQD-DAB were 21.5%, 21,2%, 55.0% and 26.2% respectively. The results are beneficial to understand the principle of the emission colors for extensive luminescent carbon quantum dots. It opens a new stratage to tune the emission colors of carbon quantum dots.

Miss Jing Li completed the synthesis of the carbon dots and the writing work. Miss Wei Wang completed the surface modification reactions of the carbon dots. Students Xin Jia, Ying-Hao Zhang and Juan-Rong Li did some synthesis work and some characterizations. Prof. Jiaqiang Xu supplied some funding for this work. I am the supervisor for this work, and I also completed the revision work of this manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support from the Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Chemistry, Shanghai Institute of Organic Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. The Innovation Project for College Students of Shanghai University from Shanghai Government, National Natural Science Foundation of China (U1704255). The authors also acknowledge the Instrumental Analysis and Research Center of Shanghai University for providing measurement services.
